questions.prompt: When does class-variance-authority (cva) pay off vs inline conditional classes?

choices.text: cva shines at 3+ variant axes (e.g., variant + size + color + state) or for components used across many places where centralized variant definitions matter. For 1-2 conditional variants (`variant === 'primary' && '...'`), inline `cn()` is cleaner and lighter. cva benefits: variants in one config, TypeScript types flow automatically (`VariantProps<typeof cvaConfig>`), defaultVariants set sensible defaults, compound variants for special combinations, boolean variants for flag props. shadcn/ui uses cva universally — pick it up to match the dominant 2026 component pattern.

questions.explanation: cva for 3+ variant axes or shared component libraries. Inline cn() for simple 1-2 variants.

questions.prompt: You want the Button's primary-color look on a Link as well. Approach without duplication?

choices.text: Export `buttonVariants` from your Button component file (the cva function itself, not just the component). Then `<Link href='/' className={buttonVariants({ variant: 'primary' })}>` applies button styles to the Link. Even better, use Radix `<Slot>` (asChild pattern) inside Button so `<Button asChild><Link href='/'>Home</Link></Button>` renders an actual <a> with all button styling and behavior. shadcn/ui exports buttonVariants alongside Button for exactly this case. Pattern: components export the variant function; consumers can apply the look without rendering the component.

questions.explanation: Export buttonVariants for class reuse; or use Radix Slot with asChild for proper composition.
